common_name: Taxi Tomato (Bright Yellow Early Determinate Tomato)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Taxi is a determinate tomato that sets its crop over a concentrated 3–4 week window before slowing. In zone 9, transplant late February through early March for a May harvest; the early to mid-season maturity makes Taxi practical for both spring and fall Gulf Coast production windows.

directions: Start seeds 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 70–80°F; Taxi is a determinate yellow tomato that produces a concentrated crop of uniformly bright taxi-yellow, medium (6–8 oz), round to slightly flattened fruits with a smooth, attractive appearance and mild, sweet, low-acid tomato flavor — the distinctive mellow sweetness typical of most yellow tomatoes, which have lower levels of the malic and citric acids that give red tomatoes their sharpness. The low-acid character makes Taxi an excellent choice for gardeners and eaters who find standard red tomatoes too acidic, for children who prefer milder flavors, and for fresh applications where the visual contrast of bright yellow adds interest to a salad or platter. The meaty flesh has a good firm texture that holds up in sliced presentations without becoming watery. Taxi is practical, productive, and early enough to complete its crop before the worst Gulf South summer heat; its concentrated set makes it useful for batch processing or sharing. A reliable, attractive yellow option for home gardens where some visual and flavor variety beyond standard red tomatoes is desired.
sources: Fedco Seeds — Taxi tomato variety notes
University of Minnesota Extension — Tomato Variety Guide

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is above 65°F; Dasher II is a gynoecious (predominantly female) cucumber requiring a male-flowering pollenizer (included in mixed seed packets) for fruit set. Train vines to a trellis for straighter fruits and better air circulation; harvest frequently at 7–8 inches for best quality and continued production.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost when soil is above 65°F; Dasher II is a gynoecious hybrid slicing cucumber that has been one of the most consistently recommended disease-resistant slicing cucumber varieties since its release — carrying resistance to cucumber mosaic virus, scab, angular leaf spot, powdery mildew, and anthracnose, providing protection against the most common disease failures in cucumber production across a wide range of climates. The dark green, 7–8 inch, slightly tapered fruits have a smooth, attractive appearance with small, even bumps and white spines; the flesh is crisp, mild, and refreshing with very low bitterness (very low cucurbitacin content), making Dasher II distinctly more palatable than older cucumber varieties that could be quite bitter. The gynoecious character concentrates fruit set into a more uniform harvest window than open-pollinated types. In humid Gulf South conditions where cucumber diseases (especially powdery mildew and angular leaf spot) severely limit cucumber production in summer, Dasher II's comprehensive disease resistance is particularly valuable, allowing productive summer growing that standard varieties cannot sustain. An excellent backbone slicer for any home garden or market garden where disease pressure is a concern.
sources: Asgrow / Ball Horticultural — Dasher II variety development
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cucumber Production Guide
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Cucumber variety comparison

common_name: Bolivian Rainbow Pepper (Ornamental-Edible Multicolor Hot Pepper)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting after last frost; Bolivian Rainbow is particularly effective as a container plant, where the compact habit and upward-pointing multicolor fruits create an outstanding ornamental display. In zone 9, it often survives as a perennial in sheltered positions, developing into a woody shrub with increasing productivity in subsequent years.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 80–85°F; Bolivian Rainbow is one of the most visually spectacular ornamental-edible peppers available — a compact plant that simultaneously displays fruits at multiple stages of ripening, cycling through purple, yellow, orange, and red all at once on the same plant, creating a stunning multicolored display that earns it a place in ornamental borders and container arrangements as much as in vegetable gardens. The plants are dense, attractive, and heavily loaded with small (1–1.5 inch), upward-pointing conical fruits; the visual effect of dozens of multicolored fruits pointing upward from the dark green foliage is genuinely striking and unlike any other common garden plant. The fruits are very hot (50,000–100,000 Scoville units), with a clean, direct heat appropriate for hot sauce, spicy condiments, or any preparation where decorative whole dried peppers provide both heat and visual interest. The purple stage is particularly ornamental (purple pigmentation rarely seen in fruiting stage of other varieties) and fades to yellow, then orange, then the final red as the fruit matures. In zone 9+, established plants often behave as perennials, developing increasingly thick, woody stems and producing heavier crops each successive season.
sources: Kitazawa Seed Company — Ornamental pepper guide
University of New Mexico Chile Pepper Institute

common_name: Facing Heaven Pepper (Chao Tian Jiao, Upward-Pointing Chinese Chile)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; in zone 9 Facing Heaven can be grown as a perennial, producing prolifically for multiple seasons from the same plant when protected from hard frost. Harvest fruits at the red stage for fresh use or drying; dried Facing Heaven peppers are essential in many Sichuan and Hunan dishes.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 78–85°F; Facing Heaven pepper (朝天椒, Cháo Tiān Jiāo, meaning 'facing heaven pepper') is one of the foundational chili pepper varieties of Chinese cuisine — a small to medium-sized (1.5–2.5 inch), bright red, upward-pointing hot pepper produced in prolific clusters where each plant can carry 50–100 fruits simultaneously, the upward orientation giving the variety its evocative name as the fruits point skyward like tiny flaming torches. The peppers are hot (30,000–70,000 Scoville units), with a clean, direct heat and a characteristic bright, slightly fruity flavor that is distinct from Mexican or Caribbean chiles — a flavor that has made this pepper type essential in the cooking of Sichuan, Hunan, Yunnan, and Guizhou provinces, where it appears in red oil (hóng yóu), chili pastes, dried whole peppers for stir-fries, and fermented condiments like dòubànjiàng. The dried bright red fruits maintain excellent color and retain their heat well; they are often dry-roasted in oil with Sichuan peppercorns as the starting point for the characteristic ma-la (numbing-hot) flavor profile of Sichuan cooking. Very productive in the Gulf South's warm climate, producing abundantly from midsummer through fall.
sources: Kitazawa Seed Company — Asian pepper guide
Sichuan Agricultural University publications on regional chile varieties
USDA National Plant Germplasm System — Capsicum annuum accessions

common_name: Gilfeather Turnip (Vermont State Vegetable, Heirloom Turnip-Rutabaga Cross)

planting.note: Direct sow in late summer for fall and winter harvest; Gilfeather turnip develops best in cool weather and improves significantly in flavor after light frosts. In zone 9, direct sow late August through September for harvest November through January. The very large roots (up to 5 pounds) require adequate space and a full growing season of cool weather for best development.

directions: Direct sow seeds thinly in well-tilled, moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil, thinning to 8–12 inches when plants are 3 inches tall; harvest in late fall after light frosts have sweetened the roots. Gilfeather turnip is one of the most historically significant and culinarily distinctive heirloom vegetables in New England — the Vermont state vegetable, named for John Gilfeather of Wardsboro, Vermont, who reportedly kept this variety secret by cutting off the tops and bottoms of roots before selling or trading them to prevent others from saving seed, maintaining a monopoly on his exceptional variety for decades in the early 20th century. The Gilfeather is not a standard turnip but appears to be a cross between turnip (Brassica rapa) and rutabaga (Brassica napobrassica), inheriting characteristics of both — the roots are large (up to 5 lbs), elongated to round with a distinctive greenish-white skin, and the white flesh is significantly sweeter, milder, and less peppery than standard turnips, with a creamy texture when cooked that many tasters prefer to either parent species. The flavor is sweet, rich, and substantial — excellent mashed, roasted, or in soups and stews — without the sharpness that can make standard turnips polarizing. A unique and genuinely outstanding heirloom vegetable with an authentic piece of New England agricultural heritage behind it; a excellent fall/winter root vegetable for zone 9 production.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Gilfeather turnip variety history
University of Vermont Extension — Gilfeather turnip
